sentences.text: I heard sounds.
I heard sounds like something popping.
Pah, pah, pah, pah, pah, pah, pah.
I-- so I-- I didn't-- I didn't really-- I didn't really associate with-- this with shooting, you know?
Because it-- it sounds differently, I don't know.
Anyway, I-- I-- I went towards the sound.
And I didn't go very far.
I saw this-- this-- this huge ditch.
And around that, a group of-- I began to hear also voices-- voices.
And I-- I saw a group of women.
And they were undressed.
And some of them were holding babies in their arms.
And-- and the-- oh.
The Germans were shooting randomly, I mean.
And they were falling.
I was so-- I was so stunned, I-- I couldn't move.
I couldn't move, I couldn't-- I was just like-- like hypnotised.
I was-- I was-- I was there.
And very soon afterwards, I-- somebody-- somebody grabbed me and-- and carried me again from there.
And that was one of the nuns, older nuns.
And they were telling me, she was telling me, the others were dead.
You were told not to venture, you were told not to go.
You have not listened to us, that's very bad.
And that night, that night our priest was back.
I don't know how they let-- let him know.
There was no telephones.
I don't know how he-- how they let him know.
But apparently, they had ways.
And he-- and we were again put into a buggy.
And-- and he drove away with us.
We were covered with all sorts of things.
There were like some boxes and stuff on the buggy.
And he-- and he drove away with us.
And after a long drive, we were-- we were-- we were brought to  a-- I remember picking from time to time-- a gate opened up.
And-- and we were-- we were inside a courtyard and in front of a large building.
And again, nuns came out and took us fast from that-- from that.
And then the priest drove away.
I don't know where.
And we were taken to-- to a room.
And, again, they gave us to-- to eat and drink.
And to-- we were taken to another room where we remained for the-- for the rest of the night.
And early in the morning, the- the older nun-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I'm trying to remember her name.
Dear God, early Alzheimer's, I think.
I forget.
[SIGH] The older nun, a Mother Superior, came and-- and took us in the morning, took us to-- to chapel.
After the-- the chapel, she sat down and told us what will be our agenda, what we are to do and my chores, and where I can go, where can I not go.
And even in the convent, there are several buildings.
We were not allowed to venture to other buildings.
We are not allowed to venture to other floors of the same building.
We were to be only in designated places every minute of the day.
Several times during our stay there, we were taken in the middle of the night.
Under-- there was-- there were like-- under the convent, there were corridors and rooms.
And we were hidden there.
And that was because Germans came to raid the convent.
They-- they were stealing from the church.
They took the organs.
They took the things that were made out of brass, out of copper.
Also, they took a lot of art things.
Some of the things were hidden also, where we were hiding, under the ground of the convent.
We-- and to this, we adhered completely.
We were-- we were watched very closely.
And so our day was 6:00 in the morning, we would go to the chapel for the-- for mass after the-- and communion later on.
And after the mass, we would-- we would come to back.
We would have breakfast.
After the breakfast, we begin-- we began our lessons.
We were tutored.
I was also singing.
So we were-- we were taught all different songs to sing, you know, the religious songs.
And that's how-- chores, our chores, and the chores we were very often doing all sorts of things in the kitchen, in the garden.
There was a garden.
I loved in the-- I loved gardens.
I just loved.
I very often, I remember, would lie down and just lay there, smelling the Earth and the-- and the flowers and everything that grew.
It seemed so peaceful and good, except it wasn't really.
So-- but that's not very long either.
We were not allowed very long to be outside.
And that's how our days-- days went by.
And pretty soon, pretty soon it became like a nature, like a second nature for me.
I then began to-- began to-- not, I have never forgotten my parents.
But I began, after years, began to put them in my mind, farther and farther.
I missed my father and my mother desperately.
And whenever I hurt, whenever I-- I wanted them to be for me.
But as time went on, it hurt less.
And I-- and I began to believe in everything that I was taught, all the saints and all this Peter, Paul.
And Paul became like a living thing.
So I didn't have anybody to play with except my sister and that not-- later on, not always that either.
So I began to-- to imagine.
I think people, too, that are alive that are actually were statues, you know, and believing with all my heart and soul that they can do things for me and that they can hear me.
And I found myself many times in solitude in church by myself.
Because I needed to talk to somebody.
They were-- the nuns were very-- very good to us.
They-- they were loving.
But they were not talkative.
They didn't talk very much.
Most of the talking was done during the tutoring, when they were teaching us reading, writing, and arithmetic.
Nothing else.
There was very little history.
I think probably purposely, because then maybe we would start asking too many questions.
As it-- as it was, I was asking a lot of questions that they never had answers for.
No.
No.
I was told not to mention that.
I-- I-- I told them what I have seen there in the first convent.
I told them.
I told them I saw-- I saw mothers and children falling into the-- being shot and falling into the-- I was told not to talk about it.
Forget about it.
Not to-- to erase from my mind.
I was told that.
And I-- and then-- and then when I was in the main convent, very little talking was done all together.
Not only to us, but even among themselves.
I didn't really see two nuns sitting and talking.
Honestly, I have not seen them sitting and kibitzing or talking about things.
I have never seen that.
They were always busy and preoccupied with chores, with survival.
Because a convent had to be self-sufficient.
There were-- they had no any kind of subsidies or anything.
I-- I for most of part, I understand that that convent was closed convent.
However, some of the nuns were teachers.
So I assume that they were going out someplace to teach somebody, in schools, I suppose.
They were preoccupied with us, only-- only what needed to be with reading.
They were gentle.
They were patient.
They were teaching.
But never, ever, anybody asked me about my mother, about my father, about my sisters, my house, nobody.
No.
There were-- yes.
I only saw one other girl.
But we heard noises that there were other children there.
And I have never seen them.
I have never seen them.
So apparently, they hid quite a few kids there.
But I-- they were so clever.
They were-- everybody was so separated that even if one would be caught, they wouldn't be able to tell anything.
Because really, we didn't know anything about anybody else.
What kind of changes were going on in you?
We're talking about some very great growth years, in terms of entering at age seven.
Formative years, most of my formative years.
I grew up to be very proper.
The-- the behavior and the etiquette was very important.
It was stressed, very important.
I was very well taught in Catholic religion.
Never, ever was mentioned anything about Jewish or Judaism or anything, even though that Christian-- the basis for Christianity is the Judaism, in Judeo-Christian.
But I never had anybody mention about that.
My days were very much alike.
One resembled the other.
I fantasized a lot.
I dreamed a lot.
I wrote a little poetry.
I wish I would have those.
I would show my nuns that were teaching me.
And I used to draw a lot and made-- just fantasized to have a lot of friends around me, people around me, you know, and make pictures of them, and give them names.
You know, and that's how-- I read a lot of poems of Mickiewicz.
That was beyond my years, but I so very-- I became so romantic.
And then when the time came, they started to-- to-- they were very outgoing when it comes to-- to education.
I mean, very limited education and limited, you know.
But in literature, for example, it was also very limited.
But I did get a hold of this poetry book.
So they finally allowed me to keep it, you know.
And a lot of things I didn't understand.
But I memorized.
I remember still things by heart that I memorized then.
And-- and then when I was a little older, I started to sing in a choir.
But the choir was behind, like, a curtain.
So people that were close could see me.
That's how my mother, when she survived, she started to look for her kids, for her family.
And she walked literally from house to house to see if anybody, anybody would see us or know anything about us.
And one woman told her that she doesn't know if, but she did see a young girl in the choir that resembles a little bit her description that what you are telling us.
And that's how she ended up coming to church.
I don't think-- that was probably the first time for my mother being in the church.
I don't think she ever was.
So she came.
That's how it started.
By then, I didn't know that.
But that was already two years-- almost two years after the war.
I had no idea that the war ended.
No-- no one really came and told me the war ended.
I had no idea.
It's about '46 or '47, about beginning.
Barbara Helena.
Well, I-- the picture that I have, that's-- that's-- that's the first picture that was made for school, for a school passport.
And that was-- that was the same year that I left the convent.
So I had to be what, 14, 13, 14, maybe even 15?
I don't know.
Yeah, 14 years.
Well, one day, priest comes to me and tells me that there is, you know, Barbara, there is a lady that lost her children.
And she is looking for her children.
And she's a very nice lady.
And she thinks that you might be one of her children.
Would you like to meet her?
And at that time, it's like a-- I-- it's so hard to-- to-- to tell you the emotions.
Because it's like somebody put an electric cord into me, like something forbidden, something unattainable, something that-- of my past that I really don't know if I wanted to.
So he noticed I didn't say anything.
But he-- well, I did say, no.
And so he didn't pursue that at that-- at that day.
But he approached me several other times on different occasions, on different days.
And at one point, I said, yes.
Because he kept telling me how-- how desperate this lady is.
She lost all her children.
And she's looking for them.
So-- so at least come and say hello to this lady and reassure her that she will find them or something, you know, at least that much.
And so I agreed.
The moment I walked into that room, it was [POLISH], that was his, like, office.
The moment I walked in, I saw a lady.
Somehow, maybe because I was so small, I remember my mother tall.
I remember my mom was very pretty, at least to me she was very beautiful.
And here I see a woman with greyish hair and bent down.
And she wore glasses.
She wore glasses.
And my mother never wore glasses.
And looked at me, and she started to talk to me.
And she says-- she looked at me.
And she apparently recognized me right away.
Because she started to say, Basha, Basha, my kin.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
I'm your mama.
I'm your mom, remember me?
Remember me?
And I remembered.
I remembered her.
You know?
With all that change, what about her did you remember?
What was still the same?
Her eyes, her dark, shiny eyes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her voice.
Her gentle voice.
[CRYING]
But I didn't want to go to her.
Isn't that awful?
I didn't want to go to her, not right then, not right then.
I-- I-- then she came another day.
And we brought my sister.
My sister, by then, looked a lot like my mom with dark, shiny eyes and jet black hair and also very pretty.
And my-- my mother, when she saw her, she really grabbed her.
Because she couldn't hold us.
So she grabbed her and embraced her.
And she started to cry, Leah started to cry.
She got so-- she got scared.
She didn't recognize my mom right away.
But surprisingly enough, it didn't take Leah as long as it took me to warm up to her back.
You know?
She didn't-- I had a lot of questions that I wanted-- that were not answer.
And Leah didn't have any questions.
She just accepted right away.
And soon after that, I think I would say about a month afterwards, we left.
I, first of all, I-- I had to have a-- I told my mom she has to promise me that she's going to convert and come to church.
My mother said, yeah, you're my kind.
[LAUGHTER] You, you're my kind.
So she would promise anything, you know.
And so we-- we-- I-- I left them.
And it was a very strange reunion, because my mother had a small apartment.
And by then, she was working.
Vilna was occupied then by Russians.
And she was working for the Russian Armies, making their uniforms or something.
And so because of that, she was given a very nice-- little, little, but very nice apartment.
And in that area that she was working, also there was one soldier, a very high rank Russian soldier, who happened to be Jewish.
His name was Blumkin.
I remember very well, because he befriended us so well and-- and helped us so much with everything.
And my mother at that time was trying to get my brother from the-- my-- my brother, meanwhile, was born in 1943, in the worst time that he could be born.
She was taken to Ponary to-- to be shot.
So on the way there, she left him-- she left him in a basket with-- with-- by the dwelling, there was a house and the forester that lived nearby there.
And knowing-- knowing that-- she didn't know that they were going to be shot.
But she had a feeling that it's not going to be any good.
And perhaps here he'll have a chance to survive this way.
If he would be going with her, then for sure he wouldn't survive.
So-- as it was, this couple, that had-- was childless couple.
They took him for their own.
And he remained there for the-- for the-- for quite some time.
And my mother-- my mother watched him.
She was-- she joined the partisans later, when she was shot.
She was wounded in Ponary and dug herself-- but not killed, as quite a few other people.
She dug herself from under the bodies and found a refuge in a nearby village.
And-- and then-- and then she joined the partisans, hoping that maybe she will find my father.
My father didn't know all-- all about this.
So and from far away, she from time to time, she watched my brother grow with this couple.
She would-- she would come back and-- and watch.
And so when the war was ended, she tried to get him out of-- she tried to get him back.
And it was very difficult.
There was-- there was a court of law.
And then they presented all documents.
And she didn't have anything.
She just knew that that was her child, and she wanted him back.
It was terrible.
So this-- this Officer Blumkin, who was in-- very high in Russian Army and intelligence, I think, he told my-- my-- my mother, Mina, don't worry.
You're going to get your son.
Even if you lose the-- because that would be by then the third time that they were trying to-- she was trying to get him.
Twice, she lost the case.
And she wouldn't give up.
So-- so this time, he told her that no matter what, they'll go and kidnap him if they have to.
And you'll have your child back.
And so but the third time, she did won the case.
And she took my brother.
It was very difficult for her.
Because my sister and I, we wanted to run to church.
My brother wanted to run back to his parents.
And she was-- she couldn't hold us all together.
So this Mr.
Blumkin, arranged for my mother with papers, like, you know-- you know, made papers for us to leave Vilnius and to go to Poland.
And to go to Poland and to Lodz specially, where there was a-- where there was a large, Jewish gathering from all over Europe.
People were coming looking for their families and looking, you know.
And so there was the largest Jewish gathering.
And so he says, you go there.
You will-- you will be safer with your kids.
And that's what-- that's how we end up in Lodz.
My sister-- my father and my sisters, we found out later on.
In Israel, we found out what happened to them.
My older sister had a friend, a high school friend.
And he was together with them in the partisans.
And he lived through that.
And he told us the story.
He told us that my father wanted to-- he knew that the Germans were retreating already.
But he wanted to come to Vilnius as soon as he possible can.
He thought maybe he can still save somebody.
He had no idea that my mom was also in partisans.
He had no idea that-- that-- where we were.
He thought we were still in the village.
And so he thought maybe he can come and gather us, and then find us and save us.
Because when the Germans were retreating, there was a-- a terrible war was going on.
And bombs were falling, and the ghetto was being destroyed and everything.
So he thought-- so the retreating Germans caught him with my sisters.
And they-- they were shot when the-- when the Germans were retreating.
So your mother raised you alone then, after that point.
Yes.
Not only did she raised us, but she also-- she also got this girl that was in convent that never knows until today-- she's alive.
She doesn't know how she got into convent.
She doesn't remember anything.
She's now about 60 years old, has two grown-- she's a grandma and lives in Israel.
But she got out from there, too.
When we came from Vilnius to-- to Lodz, with us came this little girl also.
I didn't know her really that well.
I saw her several times during our stay in convent, but we were not playing together.
She was always taken away.
And-- and we were not together.
I knew that there was-- that she was there someplace.
But we were not together.
But my mother, when the priest told-- when-- when my mother came to take us, then the priest told her that there is another Jewish child here.
And if you like to find, perhaps, her family, and she did find the family.
Her family, immediate family, were killed.
But--
Ilana-- Helina.
Yes.
Lefkowitz then.
Now it's Brudner.
She lives in Israel, in Haifa.
Yeah.
And she doesn't know.
But my mom found her relatives through newspapers.
My mom kept writing and looking forever and ever for maybe somebody is alive somewhere, someplace.
So she found her family through newspaper, her uncle in Paris.
She did go there.
And she stayed with them a little bit.
But she finally decided to come back to Israel to stay with my mom.
OK.
So.
